name: Pnoepygidae
description: Cupwings are a family of small, secretive passerine birds found in the montane forests of southern and eastern Asia. They are named for their unique cup-shaped wings and are known for their skulking behavior.

children.name: Pnoepyga
children.description: A genus of tiny, almost tailless passerines with scaled underparts, inhabiting dense undergrowth in Asian forests.

children.name: Pnoepyga pusilla
children.description: The Pygmy Cupwing is a tiny, brown bird with scalloped underparts and a very short tail. It skulks along the forest floor in montane forests from the Himalayas to Southeast Asia, feeding on small insects.

children.name: Pnoepyga albiventer
children.description: The Scaly-breasted Cupwing has dark scaly patterning on its white underparts and olive-brown upperparts. It inhabits moist montane forests from the Himalayas to southern China.

children.name: Pnoepyga immaculata
children.description: The Nepal Cupwing is a uniform brownish-grey bird with indistinct scaling on the underparts. It is endemic to the Himalayas of Nepal and adjacent India, inhabiting dense rhododendron and bamboo thickets.

children.name: Pnoepyga mutica
children.description: Found in the mountains of central and southern China, this small cupwing has olive-brown upperparts and dark scalloped underparts. It inhabits dense undergrowth in moist montane forests at elevations between 1,500 and 3,600 metres. Its distinctive song is a high-pitched, accelerating trill.

children.name: Pnoepyga formosana
children.description: Endemic to the mountains of Taiwan, this small, secretive cupwing has brown upperparts with fine pale spots and heavily scalloped underparts. It skulks on the forest floor in dense undergrowth of montane broadleaf and coniferous forests. Its song is a thin, insect-like trill that is easily overlooked.
